title: ICE Eliminates Biden-Era 30-Day Post-Release Death Reporting Requirement
excerpt: Acting ICE Director David Venturella issued a memo ending the requirement to report and investigate deaths occurring within 30 days of a detainee's release, a transparency safeguard adopted in 2021 to prevent the agency from releasing critically ill detainees to avoid in-custody death counts.
content:
On June 4, 2026, acting ICE Director David Venturella issued an internal memo eliminating ICE's requirement to report deaths occurring within 30 days of a detainee's release from custody. The Washington Post first reported the change after obtaining the memo.

  • The 2021 policy being rescinded was specifically designed to prevent ICE from releasing severely ill detainees to avoid counting their deaths as in-custody deaths — a practice critics say ICE had previously employed.
  • Former Homeland Security officials said the change removes a key accountability safeguard. Health expert Dr. Homer Venters, former chief medical officer of the NYC jail system, said the change represents "a willful act of ignoring the most serious health outcome" reflecting gaps in detention care.
  • At least 11 ICE detainees died at hospitals in 2026 after being transferred from detention facilities. Under the new policy, these deaths would no longer require investigation or reporting.
  • DHS framed the rollback as "common sense," saying ICE should not be responsible for monitoring deaths once a person leaves custody.

The policy change came amid a record-high detention death rate, with 18 people having died in ICE custody in just the first five months of 2026 — more than double the same period the previous year.
